skill.name: omero-integration
skill.description: Guides Python-based OMERO microscopy workflows, including secure connections, image retrieval, pixel analysis, ROIs, annotations, tables, and batch scripts.
skill.summary: Use OMERO Python APIs for microscopy data access, analysis, metadata, ROIs, tables, and batch processing.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution and weak-crypto findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, inline code, and ordinary OMERO examples. The skill is documentation-focused, but it covers authenticated network access, credential handling, local file writes, server-side scripts, and destructive OMERO operations, so it should publish with a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated OMERO operations can modify or delete data
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes examples for deleting projects or datasets and creating or linking images and annotations. These are legitimate OMERO workflows, but misuse can remove or alter microscopy data on a server.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples call OMERO APIs that delete objects, create images, or attach files. The risk is operational misuse rather than malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Server-side script templates execute work on OMERO servers
medium_findings.description: The skill documents OMERO.scripts that run on the OMERO server and process user-selected images or datasets. This is expected for OMERO automation, but it requires trusted scripts, parameter validation, and permission controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly describes scripts that run server-side. The examples are normal OMERO script patterns, but the execution context increases deployment risk.

low_findings.title: Environment variables are used for OMERO credentials
low_findings.description: The skill recommends reading OMERO username, password, host, and port from environment variables. This is safer than hardcoding secrets, but users should still protect shell history, logs, and runtime environments.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly read OMERO credential-related values from os.environ. This is a legitimate pattern with residual secret-handling risk.

low_findings.title: Local file export and cleanup examples touch the filesystem
low_findings.description: Examples create download directories, write annotation or original-file bytes, and remove temporary files. The usage is ordinary data export code, but generated paths should be validated in real workflows.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples show actual directory creation, binary writes, and file removal. They are legitimate but deserve a low filesystem risk note.

low_findings.title: Markdown backticks were misclassified as shell execution
low_findings.description: Most external-command findings are false positives from Markdown code fences and inline code in documentation. I found no evidence that the skill itself executes shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are Markdown file references or Python code fences, not Ruby shell backtick execution. No shell command execution intent is present.

low_findings.title: Weak-cryptography detections are false positives
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto scanner hits align with ordinary documentation text, image descriptions, HDF5 table references, and variable names. I found no evidence of hashing, encryption, or weak cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain non-cryptographic OMERO documentation and description fields. No cryptographic API or algorithm choice appears in those contexts.

low_findings.title: Critical obfuscation heuristic is not supported by context
low_findings.description: The dangerous-combination heuristic combines documentation examples for network connections, credentials, filesystem export, and code blocks. I found no obfuscation, hidden payload, or data exfiltration intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic context is a scientific OMERO documentation skill. The same primitives are legitimate for authenticated microscopy data access and export.

dangerous_patterns.title: Delete API usage requires explicit user confirmation
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples call conn.deleteObjects for projects and datasets. Marketplace users should require clear object IDs and confirmations before applying this pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly demonstrate delete operations. This is legitimate administration functionality but carries clear data-loss risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Admin impersonation workflow requires privileged controls
dangerous_patterns.description: Advanced examples show operating as another OMERO user through an admin connection. This should be limited to administrators and audited on the OMERO server.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines demonstrate switching to another user through an admin connection. Legitimate OMERO administration is possible, but authorization must be enforced externally.

content.user_title: Analyze OMERO microscopy data with Python
content.value_statement: Microscopy teams need repeatable access to OMERO images, metadata, ROIs, and analysis results. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through practical Python patterns for OMERO workflows.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to connect to OMERO servers with BlitzGateway and managed sessions.
Explains how to retrieve projects, datasets, images, screens, plates, wells, and metadata.
Documents pixel access, rendering settings, derived image creation, and NumPy-based analysis patterns.
Covers ROI creation, retrieval, coordinate export, and intensity measurement workflows.
Guides annotation, file attachment, OMERO table, and batch script workflows.
limitations: Requires an available OMERO server, valid credentials, and the omero-py dependency.
Provides documentation and examples, not a packaged connector or live server client.
Destructive and administrative operations still depend on OMERO permissions and user approval.
Large image workflows may require server-side scripts, tiling, or batch processing to control memory use.

use_cases.title: Support shared OMERO repositories
use_cases.description: Find datasets, inspect metadata, add annotations, and export files for researchers using consistent Python examples.

use_cases.title: Measure image regions
use_cases.description: Retrieve images and ROIs, calculate pixel statistics, and store measurements back into OMERO tables.

use_cases.title: Automate plate analysis
use_cases.description: Navigate screens, plates, wells, and image sets while designing batch scripts for repeatable analysis.

prompt_templates.title: List my OMERO projects
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me a safe Python pattern to connect to OMERO with BlitzGateway and list my projects and datasets.

prompt_templates.title: Retrieve image pixels
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me retrieve an OMERO image by ID, read its pixel data as NumPy arrays, and summarize channel and Z dimensions.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze ROIs and save results
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a workflow that reads ROIs from OMERO images, measures intensity by channel, and stores results in an OMERO table.

prompt_templates.title: Build a batch OMERO script
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft an OMERO.scripts plan for batch processing images from a dataset, validating parameters, reporting progress, and returning result objects.

output_examples.input: Connect to OMERO and list available datasets.
output: A connection plan using BlitzGateway with automatic cleanup.
A checklist for host, port, username, password, and secure connection settings.
A dataset listing approach that handles empty results and connection failures.
A step-by-step workflow for fetching images and ROI shapes.
Guidance on extracting channel-specific pixel values from each region.
A result storage plan using OMERO tables or file annotations.

output_examples.input: Prepare a batch script for high-content screening images.
output: A server-side script structure with validated parameters.
A batching strategy for screens, plates, wells, and images.
Operational notes for progress messages, memory control, and cleanup.

best_practices: Use context managers and close OMERO sessions after each workflow.
Load credentials from protected environment variables or secure configuration stores.
Confirm object IDs and permissions before creating, linking, deleting, or impersonating objects.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode OMERO passwords, session IDs, or production host details in shared scripts.
Do not run delete or admin impersonation examples without explicit approval and audit logging.
Do not load full large images into memory when tiling or server-side processing is available.

faq.question: What is OMERO?
faq.answer: OMERO is an open-source platform for managing, viewing, and analyzing microscopy images and metadata.

faq.question: Does this skill connect to my server directly?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Your runtime environment must have network access, credentials, and omero-py installed.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Can it help with ROIs?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers creating, retrieving, exporting, and measuring OMERO regions of interest.

faq.question: Can it store analysis results?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents OMERO tables, annotations, file attachments, and derived images.

faq.question: Is it safe to use on production OMERO data?
faq.answer: Use it carefully. Review destructive, administrative, and server-side script examples before running them on production data.
